content:
**Matter** is anything that has mass and occupies space — iron, water, air, wood, oil, steam.

• An **element** is a substance that cannot be broken into simpler substances; its smallest unit is an **atom**.
• A **molecule** is the smallest unit of matter that can exist independently and shows all the properties of that matter. A molecule may consist of one or more atoms (a common-salt molecule has one sodium and one chlorine atom).

**Must know — properties of molecules**
• Molecules of a given substance are **identical** in shape, size and mass.
• Molecules are in a state of **continuous random motion** and therefore possess kinetic energy.
• Molecules attract one another with **intermolecular forces**:
  – **Cohesion**: attraction between molecules of the **same** substance (water–water; it pulls two touching water drops into one).
  – **Adhesion**: attraction between molecules of **different** substances (glue–paper, water–glass, ink–paper).
• These forces weaken rapidly as the distance between molecules increases.
explanation:
Everyday evidence:
• Two water drops merge on touching — cohesion.
• Glue sticks two sheets of paper — adhesion.
• Chalk powders easily, brick is harder to break, iron is hardest — cohesion is weakest in chalk, stronger in brick, very strong in iron.
examTip: Cohesion = same substance, adhesion = different substances — examiners love swapping the two in true/false questions.

content:
Matter exists as **solid, liquid and gas**. The differences come from intermolecular **forces** and **spaces**.

**Solids**
• Molecules closely packed; intermolecular spaces negligible; forces strongest.
• Molecules only **vibrate about fixed positions** → definite shape AND definite volume; practically incompressible.

**Liquids**
• Spaces slightly larger than solids; forces weaker — molecules **slide past one another**.
• Definite volume but **no definite shape** (takes the container's shape); has a **definite free surface**; only slightly compressible.

**Gases**
• Spaces very large; forces negligible — each molecule moves **independently at high speed**.
• Neither definite shape nor definite volume; fills the whole vessel; **no free surface**; highly compressible; can only be stored in a closed vessel.

**Order to remember**
• Intermolecular force: solid > liquid > gas.
• Intermolecular space: gas > liquid > solid.
• Compressibility: gas > liquid > solid.
explanation: Why compression works: squeezing reduces the EMPTY space between molecules. Gases have huge spaces, so they compress easily; in solids the molecules already touch, so there is almost nothing to squeeze.
examTip: Write the three 'order chains' (force, space, compressibility) at the top of your answer sheet — half the MCQs on this chapter test them.

content:
**Main assumptions of the kinetic theory**
• Matter is made of tiny particles (molecules); molecules of one substance are identical.
• Molecules are in **continuous random motion** that never stops — so they possess kinetic energy.
• The **average kinetic energy of molecules increases with temperature** (temperature is a measure of molecular kinetic energy).
• Molecules attract each other (cohesion/adhesion); the attraction weakens with distance.
• Between molecules there are empty **intermolecular spaces**.

**The theory explains**
• **Solids keep their shape** — molecules vibrate but cannot leave fixed positions.
• **Diffusion** — perfume spreads across a room because vapour molecules wander randomly through the spaces between air molecules.
• **Gas pressure** — billions of molecular collisions per second on every wall of the container push outward equally in all directions.
• **Energy order of states**: molecules of a gas have the most energy, of a solid the least (for the same substance, steam > water > ice).
explanation: Common misconception: 'molecules stop moving in a solid'. They never stop — they vibrate in place. Motion stops for nothing; even in ice the molecules vibrate about fixed positions.
examTip: Any 'explain using kinetic theory' answer should mention BOTH intermolecular force and intermolecular space — one alone loses marks.

subtopic: 4. Change of State, Evaporation and Boiling
content:
A **change of state** is a physical change — molecules never change, only their spacing, forces and energy.

• **Melting (fusion)**: solid → liquid at a fixed **melting point** (pure ice: 0 °C).
• **Vaporisation**: liquid → gas, by evaporation or boiling.
• **Condensation**: gas → liquid; **releases** latent heat (dew, droplets on a cold glass).
• **Freezing**: liquid → solid (reverse of melting).
• **Sublimation**: solid → vapour directly (camphor, iodine, naphthalene balls).

**Latent heat**: during melting or boiling the temperature stays CONSTANT — the heat supplied is used to overcome intermolecular forces, not to speed up molecules.

**Evaporation vs boiling**
• Evaporation: at ALL temperatures, only from the **surface**, slow, causes **cooling**.
• Boiling: at ONE fixed temperature (boiling point, water 100 °C), throughout the **bulk**, rapid.

**Evaporation increases with**: larger surface area, higher temperature, wind, lower humidity. (Depth/quantity of liquid does NOT matter.)

**Evaporation cools** because the most energetic molecules escape, lowering the average kinetic energy of those left behind — sweating, earthen pots, sprinkled water, sanitizer on skin.
explanation:
Why steam burns worse than boiling water: both are at 100 °C, but steam additionally carries the latent heat of vaporisation, which it releases on condensing on the skin.

Why ice cools a drink better than ice-cold water: melting ice absorbs latent heat of fusion in addition to warming up.
examTip: For 'give reason' questions on cooling (matka, sweating, sanitizer), always name BOTH evaporation AND the latent heat drawn from the body/liquid.

content:
**Density** = mass ÷ volume — the mass packed into unit volume of a substance.

• SI unit: **kg/m³**; CGS unit: **g/cm³**; conversion: **1 g/cm³ = 1000 kg/m³**.
• Density of water = 1 g/cm³ = 1000 kg/m³ (at 4 °C).
• Density is a **characteristic property**: a small piece and a large piece of the same pure substance have the SAME density — so density helps identify a substance.
• On heating, a substance expands: same mass, larger volume → **density decreases**.

**Relative density (R.D.)** = density of substance ÷ density of water.
• It is a pure **ratio — no unit**.
• R.D. of iron ≈ 7.8 means iron is 7.8 times as dense as water.

**Useful approximate relative densities**: cork 0.25, wood 0.6–0.8, kerosene 0.8, ice 0.9, water 1.0, milk 1.03, sea water 1.025, aluminium 2.7, iron/steel 7.8, mercury 13.6.
explanation: Equal volumes of iron and wood: iron is heavier (more mass per cm³). Equal masses of iron and cotton: cotton occupies far more volume. Both statements are just density read in two directions.
examTip: In conversions, multiply g/cm³ by 1000 to get kg/m³ — and remember relative density NEVER carries a unit.

content:
Density needs two measurements: **mass** (beam balance) and **volume**.

**Regular solid** (cube, cuboid, cylinder)
• Volume from dimensions: V = l × b × h, measured with a metre scale.

**Irregular solid that sinks** (stone, metal piece)
• Use the **displacement method**: a fully submerged solid displaces water equal to its own volume.
• With a measuring cylinder: volume = final reading − initial reading.
• With a **eureka (displacement) can**: fill to the spout, lower the solid by a thread, collect and measure the overflow.

**Irregular solid lighter than water** (cork, wax)
• It floats, so tie it to a **sinker** (a heavy metal piece).
• Measure volume with sinker alone submerged (V₁), then sinker + solid submerged (V₂).
• Volume of the solid = V₂ − V₁.

Then **density = mass ÷ volume**.
explanation: Precautions: the solid must be fully submerged; lower it gently (no splashing); read the measuring cylinder at the lower meniscus at eye level; the thread's volume is negligible. Never push a floating solid down with a finger — the finger's volume adds to the reading.
examTip: For 'describe an experiment' answers, write the steps in order — mass first, volume by displacement, then the formula. The sinker idea is the favourite twist.

content:
**Law of floatation**: a body floats in a liquid if its density is **less than or equal to** the density of the liquid; it sinks if its density is greater. A floating body displaces liquid whose **weight equals its own weight**.

**Applications and examples**
• **Ice/iceberg** floats with about **9/10 of its volume submerged** (R.D. of ice ≈ 0.9) — why icebergs endanger ships.
• **Steel ship floats** though steel sinks: the hollow hull encloses air, so the AVERAGE density of (steel + air) is less than water.
• **Submarine**: floods ballast tanks with sea water to increase average density and dive; blows the water out with compressed air to rise.
• **Egg in brine**: an egg sinks in fresh water but floats when salt raises the water's density.
• **Sea vs river**: swimming is easier in denser sea water (greater upthrust).
• **Oil/kerosene** floats on water — lower density and immiscible.

**Hydrometer**: a weighted, sealed float with a graduated stem that measures liquid density directly — based on floatation. It **sinks deeper in a less dense liquid**. A **lactometer** is a hydrometer for milk: added water lowers the milk's density, so the lactometer sinks deeper.
explanation: Why a hydrometer's bulb is heavy and stem thin: the weighted bulb keeps it floating upright; the thin stem makes a small density change give a large, readable change in depth (sensitivity). Scale readings increase DOWNWARD on the stem — denser liquid, higher float.
examTip: 'Floats in A, sinks in B' ⇒ density of A ≥ body > density of B. Practise this one-line logic — it appears in every Olympiad paper.

content:
**The three forms of one formula**
• density = mass ÷ volume
• mass = density × volume
• volume = mass ÷ density

**Worked patterns**
• Displacement: metal of mass 540 g raises a cylinder reading from 60 to 260 cm³ → V = 200 cm³ → density = 540/200 = **2.7 g/cm³** (aluminium).
• Mass from density: V = 25 cm³, density 8 g/cm³ → mass = 8 × 25 = **200 g**.
• From relative density: R.D. 0.8 → density = 0.8 × 1000 = **800 kg/m³**.
• Float or sink: mass 200 g, volume 250 cm³ → density 0.8 g/cm³ < 1 → **floats**.

**Checklist**
• Keep units consistent: g with cm³, kg with m³.
• 1 g/cm³ = 1000 kg/m³; 1 mL = 1 cm³.
• Always compare densities (never masses) to decide floating/sinking.
explanation: Most errors come from mixing unit systems mid-calculation. Convert FIRST, then substitute. State the unit in every line of working — ICSE awards method marks.
examTip: Show formula → substitution → answer with unit, in three separate lines; missing units lose a mark even with the right number.

subtopic: 1. Force: A Push or a Pull
content:
A **force** is a push or a pull that can change a body's state of rest or motion, its speed, its direction, or its shape and size.

• SI unit: **newton (N)**.
• **Contact forces** act only on touching: muscular force, friction, normal reaction.
• **Non-contact forces** act at a distance: gravitational, magnetic, electrostatic.

A force can produce two kinds of effect on a rigid body:
• **Translational motion** — moving the body bodily from one place to another.
• **Rotational (turning) motion** — turning it about an axis or pivot (a door about its hinges, a wheel about its axle). The turning effect is studied as the **moment of force**.
explanation: This chapter builds on the idea of force from earlier classes and then concentrates on two new quantities: moment of force (turning) and pressure (force spread over area).
examTip: Classify forces in two boxes — contact and non-contact — with two examples each; a one-mark classification appears almost every year.

subtopic: 2. Turning Effect of Force: Moment and Couple
content:
**Moment of force** (turning effect) about an axis:

• **Moment = force × perpendicular distance** of the force's line of action from the axis.
• SI unit: **newton metre (N m)** — written N m, never joule, because moment is not work.
• It can be **clockwise** or **anticlockwise**.
• A force passing THROUGH the axis has zero perpendicular distance → **zero turning effect**.

**To get a large turning effect with a small force, apply it far from the axis:**
• Door handle fixed at the edge farthest from the hinges.
• A long spanner loosens a tight nut easily.
• A long steering wheel radius, bicycle handlebars, see-saw seats at the ends.

**Couple**: two equal, parallel, OPPOSITE forces acting at different points — net force is zero, but both moments act in the SAME rotational direction, so the body rotates.
• Examples: turning a steering wheel, winding a key, opening a tap, turning a screwdriver.
explanation: Why both moments of a couple add: if both forces turn the wheel clockwise, their turning effects reinforce even though the forces themselves cancel as pushes.
examTip: Always convert the distance to metres before computing a moment (40 cm = 0.4 m) — the classic numerical trap.

subtopic: 3. Thrust and Pressure — Solids, Liquids, Atmosphere
content:
**Thrust** = force acting normally (perpendicular) on a surface, in newtons.
**Pressure** = thrust ÷ area; SI unit **pascal (Pa)** = 1 N/m².

**Same force, smaller area → larger pressure**
• Cutting/piercing tools are sharpened: knife edge, nail tip, needle.
**Same force, larger area → smaller pressure**
• Broad school-bag straps, camel's broad feet on sand, skis on snow, wide building foundations, tank caterpillar tracks, porter's head pad, broad sleepers under rails.

**Liquid pressure: P = h ρ g** (depth × density × gravity)
• Increases with **depth** — dams are thicker at the base; the lowest hole in a tank spurts farthest; deep-sea divers need strengthened suits.
• Increases with **density** — sea water presses more than fresh water at the same depth.
• Does **NOT** depend on the shape, width or volume of the vessel.
• At a point inside a liquid, pressure acts **equally in all directions**.

**Atmospheric pressure** — the weight of the air column above us (~10⁵ Pa at sea level; measured with a **barometer**).
• Acts equally in all directions; decreases with altitude (mountaineers' nose-bleeds).
• Explains: drinking straw, syringe, dropper, rubber sucker — 'suction' is really the atmosphere PUSHING liquid toward a low-pressure region.
• We are not crushed because the pressure of air and fluids inside our body balances it.
explanation:
Three formulas to keep apart:
• Pressure on a surface: P = F/A.
• Pressure at depth h in a liquid: P = hρg.
• Every 10 m of water adds about one atmosphere (10 m × 1000 kg/m³ × 10 m/s² = 10⁵ Pa).
examTip: 'Liquid pressure depends on the vessel's shape' is FALSE — the most repeated assertion-reason trick in this chapter.

subtopic: 4. A Liquid Seeks Its Own Level
content:
In interconnected vessels — whatever their shapes or widths — a liquid at rest stands at the **same horizontal level** in all of them.

**Why:** at the connected base, points at the same depth must have the same pressure. If one column stood higher, the pressure under it would be greater and liquid would flow until the levels equalised. Since liquid pressure depends only on depth (not vessel shape), equal pressure forces equal heights.

**Everyday examples**
• Tea stands at the same level in a teapot and its spout.
• A water-level tube (transparent hose) used by masons to mark equal heights across a room.
• Water tanks and supply pipes — water rises in house pipes to the level of the overhead reservoir.
explanation: This is a direct consequence of P = hρg: same liquid, same depth ⇒ same pressure; any difference in level creates a pressure difference that drives flow until it disappears.
examTip: Draw the classic vases-of-different-shapes diagram with one dotted horizontal line through all the surfaces — the line IS the answer.

content:
**Formulas**
• Moment = F × d (N m); d is the PERPENDICULAR distance in metres.
• Pressure = F ÷ A (Pa); A in m².
• Liquid pressure = h ρ g (Pa); h in m, ρ in kg/m³, g ≈ 10 m/s².

**Worked patterns**
• Moment: 2.5 N at 40 cm from the nut → 2.5 × 0.4 = **1 N m**.
• Pressure: 400 N on 0.5 m² → 400 ÷ 0.5 = **800 Pa** (dividing by a fraction makes the answer bigger).
• Liquid: depth 10 m of water → 10 × 1000 × 10 = **100,000 Pa** = 10⁵ Pa ≈ 1 atmosphere.

**Checklist**
• cm → m before substituting.
• Use the perpendicular distance, not just any distance.
• For liquid pressure, the depth is measured from the FREE SURFACE down to the point.
explanation: If an answer looks absurdly large or small, check the unit conversion first — cm² to m² (divide by 10,000) is the most-missed step in pressure problems.
examTip: Memorise '10 m of water ≈ 1 atm ≈ 10⁵ Pa' — it lets you sanity-check every hρg answer instantly.

content:
**Work** is done when a force moves a body: **W = force × displacement in the direction of the force**.

• SI unit: **joule (J)**; 1 J = 1 N × 1 m.
• TWO conditions: a force must act AND the body must be displaced along (a component of) the force.
• **Zero-work cases**:
  – Pushing a rigid wall (no displacement).
  – A coolie standing still with a load (no displacement).
  – Carrying a bag while walking on level ground — the supporting force is vertical, displacement horizontal: perpendicular ⇒ zero work by that force.
• Work and energy are **scalar** quantities (magnitude only).

**Energy** is the capacity to do work; also measured in **joules**. A body that can do work possesses energy: a moving hammer (kinetic), stored water (potential), a stretched bow (elastic potential), fuel (chemical).
explanation: 'Feeling tired' is not the physics definition of work — your muscles consume energy holding a load, but if nothing moves, no work is done ON the load. This distinction is the single most-tested idea of the chapter.
examTip: Before answering 'how much work?', ask two questions: Is there a force? Did its point of application move along the force? If either answer is no, W = 0.

content:
**Kinetic energy** — energy of motion: **KE = ½ m v²**.
• Doubling the speed makes KE **four times** (v is squared) — why braking distances grow so fast.
• Examples: moving car, flowing water, wind, a struck hammer.

**Potential energy** — energy of position or shape:
• **Gravitational PE = m g h** (proportional to height): water in a dam or overhead tank, a raised hammer, a brick on a shelf.
• **Elastic PE** (changed shape): stretched bow or rubber band, compressed spring, wound clock spring.

**Interconversion (conservation of mechanical energy)**
• Falling body: PE → KE; at half the height, KE = PE = half the initial PE; just before the ground, all KE.
• Ball thrown up: KE → PE; at the top KE = 0, PE maximum.
• **Pendulum**: extremes — all PE; lowest (mean) point — all KE; total PE + KE constant. It finally stops only because friction and air resistance convert the energy to heat — energy is never destroyed.

**Law of conservation of energy**: energy can neither be created nor destroyed, only changed from one form to another.

**Energy chains**: torch (chemical → electrical → light + heat); hydroelectric station (PE of water → KE → turbine rotation → electrical); microphone (sound → electrical); loudspeaker (electrical → sound); solar cell (light → electrical).
explanation: KE depends on v²; PE depends on h (linearly). So double speed → 4× KE, but double height → only 2× PE. Keep the two scalings separate — mixing them is the classic MCQ error.
examTip: For any 'trace the energy changes' question, write the chain with arrows and name every intermediate form — each named form carries a mark.

content:
**Power** is the RATE of doing work (or of converting energy):

• **Power = work ÷ time**.
• SI unit: **watt (W)**; 1 W = 1 J/s. Bigger units: 1 kW = 1000 W; 1 MW = 10⁶ W.

**Energy vs power — keep them apart**
• Energy answers 'HOW MUCH work can be done?' (joule).
• Power answers 'HOW FAST is it done?' (watt).
• Two boys doing the same climb do EQUAL work; the faster one develops MORE power.
• A powerful machine need not do more total work — a weaker machine running longer can do more.

**Quick patterns**
• 600 J in 20 s → P = 30 W.
• A 40 kg girl climbing 5 m in 10 s: work = mgh = 2000 J; power = 200 W.
explanation: The horse-power (hp ≈ 746 W) survives in motor ratings, but ICSE answers should be in watts. For electrical appliances, the power rating in watts is this same physics quantity — the link to the electricity chapter.
examTip: If a question gives a TIME, it is a power question; if it does not, it is a work/energy question — sort first, then pick the formula.

content:
**Formulas**
• W = F × d (J)
• PE = m g h (take g = 10 m/s² unless told otherwise)
• KE = ½ m v² — square the speed FIRST
• P = W ÷ t (W)

**Worked patterns**
• 50 N moves a box 4 m → W = 200 J.
• 2 kg book to a 1.5 m shelf → PE = 2 × 10 × 1.5 = 30 J.
• 0.5 kg ball at 4 m/s → KE = ½ × 0.5 × 16 = 4 J.
• 600 J in 20 s → P = 30 W.
• Staircase: work uses the VERTICAL height only, never the slant length of the stairs.

**Checklist**
• Units: mass kg, distance m, time s, speed m/s.
• Work against gravity = mgh, independent of the path taken.
• Energy and work share the joule; power alone is the watt.
explanation: Two classic traps: forgetting to square v in KE, and using the staircase length instead of vertical height for mgh. Check both before writing the final answer.
examTip: Write the formula line first even in MCQ rough work — substituting into a written formula almost eliminates careless errors.
